# CSCE 608 — Course Syllabus (Spring 2026)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 608.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 608 Syllabus
Section 600 (46648)
Database Systems
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information
Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: MWF
Start Time: 3:00PM
End Time: 3:50PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location:  HRBB 113
Credit Hours:  3
[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Jianer Chen
Email:  j-chen3@tamu.edu
Office Location:  PETR 428
Phone:  (979) 845-4259
Office Hours
MWF 10:10 am - 11:10 am, PETR 428
[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Database Systems. (3-0). Credit 3. Database modeling techniques; expressiveness in
query languages including knowledge representation; manipulation languages data
models; physical data organization; relational database design theory; query
processing; transaction management and recovery; distributed data management.
Prerequisite: CSCE 310 or approval of Instructor.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 310 or approval of Instructor.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
understand the fundamentals of database design
develop database systems for applications
understand relational database theory
master efficient query processing techniques
deal with system issues related to database systems
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Required
Database Systems: The Complete Book
ISBN:  978-0-13-187325-4
Authors:  H. Garcia-Molina, J. Ullman, and J. Widom
Publisher:  Pearson Prentice Hall
Publication Date:  2009
Edition:  2nd
This material Is: Recommended
Supplementary reading materials
Authors:  The instructor Jianer Chen
Notes:
posted in Canvas
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
The course has 3 homework assignments, 2 course projects, a midterm exam,
and a final exam.
Exams are taken in-class.
Homework: 6*3 = 18%, Projects: 22+15 = 37%, Midterm Exam: 15%, Final Exam:
30%.
Grading Scale: A = 90-100%, B = 70-89%, C = 60-69%, F = 0-59%.
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
[12.0pt] The homework and project assignments are due on the designated due dates
[12.0pt bold] at the beginning of class . No late submissions will be accepted. Discuss
[12.0pt] unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
Week 1 (1/12- ): Introduction to database systems
Week 2 (1/19- ): Database design
Week 3 (1/26- ): Functional dependency and relation normalization
Week 4 (2/2- ): SQL
Week 5 (2/9- ): SQL (continued), homework #1 due
Week 6 (2/16- ): Constraints and triggers
Week 7 (2/23- ): Memory hierarchy
Week 8 (3/2- ): Query optimization, homework #2 due, midterm exam (3/6)
Week 9 (3/9- ): Spring Break. No classes
Week 10 (3/16- ): Query optimization (continued), course project #1 due
Week 11 (3/23- ): Index structures
Week 12 (3/30- ): System crash recovery
Week 13 (4/6- ): System crash recovery (continued), homework #3 due
Week 14 (4/13- ): Concurrency control
Week 15 (4/20- ): Transaction processing
Week 16 (4/27): Bigdata, course summary, course project #2 due.
May 4, Monday, 10:30am–12:30pm: Final exam
[16.0pt] Additional Course Information
All course information will be posted in canvas. You should check your course
announcements, course material and due dates daily. It is strongly
recommended that you keep up with the course material and do not fall
behind. Start working on assignments as soon as they are assigned.
This course is using Canvas Learning Management System. Students submit
their homework and projects via Canvas.
[16.0pt] AI Statement
The use of AI is strictly prohibited in this course.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Attendance Policy
Class attendance is strongly recommended. The student is responsible for any
material missed. Missed exams may be made up only when the absence is
university sanctioned. Medical reasons or other extenuating conditions beyond the
control of the student must be properly documented. Discuss unusual
circumstances in advance with the instructor when possible.

# CSCE 611 — Course Syllabus (Spring 2026)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 611.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 611 Syllabus
Section 600 (50668)
Operating Systems
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information
Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: TR
Start Time: 5:30PM
End Time: 6:45PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026

[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Riccardo Bettati

Phone:  (979) 845-5469
Office Hours
zoom: W 4pm-5pm; in-person F 11am-12pm
Preferred Contact Method
piazza and email
[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Operating Systems. (3-0). Credit 3. Review of computer architecture
hardware/software evolution leading to contemporary operating systems; basic
operating systems concepts; methods of operating systems design and
construction; algorithms for CPU scheduling memory and general resource
allocation; process coordination and management; case studies of several
operating systems; quality-of-services of operating systems and their impact on
applications. Only one of the following will satisfy the requirements for a degree:
CSCE 410 or CSCE 611. Prerequisites: CSCE 313; graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Additional Course Details
[12.0pt] This course gives an overview of the general architecture and the most
[12.0pt] important components of modern operating systems. The student will
[12.0pt] leverage knowledge acquired in previous courses on Computer Architecture
[12.0pt] and Computer Systems to understand (and to some level implement)
[12.0pt] aspects of operating systems, such as memory management, persistent
[12.0pt] storage and file systems, threading, scheduling, and resource management
[12.0pt] in general. The student will understand fundamental approaches to
[12.0pt] virtualization and what it takes to build a distributed system. The course will
[12.0pt] cover general aspects, realization approaches, and case studies. In addition,
[12.0pt] the student will implement a simple operating system that will be able to boot
[12.0pt] and run on a bare PC.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 313; graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Describe and compare the role, the architecture, and the major components of
an operating system.
Describe the role and operation of exception and interrupt handling in modern
operating systems.
Implement components of a virtual memory manager for a paged system.
Evaluate and compare various forms of concurrency mechanisms.
Evaluate and compare and apply various synchronization mechanisms, both
for single-processor and multiprocessor systems.
Implement components of a threading system for a single-processor system.
Implement components of a simple device driver.
Implement components of a simple file system.
Evaluate and compare the various virtualization approaches and their effect on
system performance.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None

This material Is: Required

[16.0pt] Grading Policy
[12.0pt bold] The course will have a  midterm  and a  final exam  (totaling 400 points). In
[12.0pt bold] addition, there will be a series of  design and programming
[12.0pt bold] assignments  (machine problems) that will walk the students through the
[12.0pt] steps of designing and developing a simple operating system (400 points).
[12.0pt bold] Finally, there will be a set of  daily quizzes  (100 points) and a set
[12.0pt bold] of  homework assignments  (100 points).
[12.0pt] The course will be using a standard letter grading scale.
[12.0pt] Standard Letter Grading Scale:
[12.0pt] A = 90% and above
[12.0pt] B = 80% and above
[12.0pt] C = 70% and above
[12.0pt] D = 60% and above
[12.0pt] F = less than 60%
[12.0pt bold] Note  that the total score for daily quizzes and for homework assignments
[12.0pt] may not sum up to exactly 100 points each by the end of the semester, but
[12.0pt] rather be off by a few points. If this happens, we will re-weigh the total score
[12.0pt] at the end of the semester to make it sum up to 100 points. As a result, the
[12.0pt] total score as indicated by Canvas may be slightly off.
[12.0pt bold] Submission of Assignments
[12.0pt] Homework assignments and machine problems will be posted and submitted
[12.0pt] on Canvas. It is the student’s responsibility to make sure that the correct
[12.0pt] assignment is submitted to the correct place. Also, Canvas submissions may
[12.0pt bold] look completed when they actually are not.  It is the student’s
[12.0pt bold] responsibility to make sure that the submission process is completed.
[12.0pt] It is best to download the submission and to confirm that the submission
[12.0pt] stored on Canvas is the intended one.
[12.0pt bold] Under no circumstances  will the instructor team consider material that has
[12.0pt] not been part of the original submission. If a student wants to add to or
[12.0pt] correct submitted files because they noticed that the original submission was
[12.0pt] wrong or incomplete, they can do this, but this will be considered as a new
[12.0pt] submission, and any late penalty will apply. The instructor and the TA’s will
[12.0pt] not consider non-submitted material, and they will not consider the file
[12.0pt] timestamps (as opposed to submission timestamps) as indication of
[12.0pt] completion of the assignment. (Time stamps of files can be easily tampered
[12.0pt] with and will therefore not be considered.) In some special cases we will
[12.0pt] consider GitHub timestamps to reconsider machine problem submissions.
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
[12.0pt] Deadlines for on-line quizzes and homework assignments will be hard.
[12.0pt] (Quizzes will disappear after the due date.) Late submissions will not be
[12.0pt] accepted. Unless noted otherwise, lateness for machine problems is
[12.0pt] penalized with 1/5 of the earned points per calendar day (1/3 during 10-week
[12.0pt] summer courses). Lateness penalty starts at the deadline and is pro-rated.
[12.0pt] This means that the student will incur a penalty of approximately 0.0139
[12.0pt] percent for each minute that their submission is late (0.0231 percent during
[12.0pt bold] 10-week summer courses).  No make-up work submissions will be
[12.0pt bold] accepted.

[16.0pt] Additional Course Information
[14.0pt bold] Academic Integrity:
[12.0pt] “An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do.”
[12.0pt] The most important value of this course is in its power of "demystification":
[12.0pt] You will experience a number of "aha, this is how it works!" moments, which
[12.0pt] are most valuable, and enjoyed best, when you experience that discovery
[12.0pt] through your own work, rather than copying somebody else's.
[12.0pt] You are encouraged to discuss solution approaches to programming
[12.0pt] assignments. The solution that you hand in has to be your own, however.
[12.0pt] This means that you are not to use any part (code, text, figures, etc.) that
[12.0pt] has been developed by others, including AI. Spelled out, this means:
[12.0pt] For programming assignments: Do not show or make available in any
[12.0pt] form to other students either design documents or code. Do not look at or
[12.0pt] use other students’ design documents or code. Do not make use of
[12.0pt] previous years’ solutions. Do not look at or download solutions from the
[12.0pt] Internet. Do not submit or make use of AI generated solutions.
[12.0pt] For homework: Do not make your homework available to other students.
[12.0pt] Do not copy from other students’ work. Do not look at or download
[12.0pt] solutions from the Internet. Do not submit or make use of AI generated
[12.0pt] solutions.
[12.0pt] For exams: Do not use any information from sources other than the
[12.0pt] exam form and allowed cheat sheets; disallowed sources of information
[12.0pt] include the Internet, books, notes, AI tools, or other students. Do not
[12.0pt] pass any information to other students. By information we mean any
[12.0pt] information, not even the time-of-day.
[12.0pt] How do we determine that plagiarism has happened? We will delegate our
[12.0pt] decisions to code-similarity detection tools and automated plagiarism
[12.0pt] detection tools. Whenever a tool flags your submission, we will treat it as a
[12.0pt] potential violation, and we will issue a report to the Aggie Honor System
[12.0pt] Office. So, to be safe, stay away from other students’ code and other
[12.0pt] solutions that are not your own!
[12.0pt] Once an alleged violation has been reported, it is Aggie Honor Systems
[12.0pt] Office policy to not allow the student to Q-drop the course until the student is
[12.0pt] cleared of the violation.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Attendance Policy
[12.0pt bold] Classroom: In-person sections  will meet during scheduled class times in
[12.0pt] the assigned room.
[12.0pt bold] Exams dates  are scheduled at the beginning of the semester, and make-up
[12.0pt] exams are given only in extraordinary circumstances. Two such cases are:
[12.0pt bold] Medical:  If a student cannot take an exam because of medical reasons,
[12.0pt] a doctor’s note is required in order to schedule a make-up exam (See
[12.0pt] Student Rule 7 for details).
[12.0pt bold] Mandatory Interviews:  If a student has to schedule a makeup exam
[12.0pt] because of a “mandatory” interview for employment or professional or
[12.0pt bold] graduate school, she or he has to submit  a written note from the
[12.0pt bold] organizer of the interview  stating that the interview is in fact mandatory
[12.0pt] and cannot be held at a different time. This note must be submitted to the
[12.0pt bold] instructor  at least 2 weeks before  the interview, and it must contain the
[12.0pt] contact information, including email and telephone number, of the person
[12.0pt] responsible for the interview. The instructor will then contact the
[12.0pt] organizer and confirm that the interview cannot be rescheduled.
[12.0pt] The university views class attendance and participation as an individual
[12.0pt] student responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete
[12.0pt] all assignments.
[12.0pt] Please refer to  Student Rule 7  in its entirety for information about excused
[12.0pt] absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

[16.0pt] CSCE 612 Syllabus

Applied Networks and Distributed Processing
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Applied Networks and Distributed Processing. (3-0). Credit 3. Fundamentals,
including network design and protocol analysis, in the context of computer
communications; mixes fundamentals with both programming and pragmatic views
of engineering issues; it includes network architecture as well as principles of
network engineering; focus is on applying principles of layered architecture to
analyzing real networks; lab exercises focus on protocol understanding and
programming; knowledge of UNIX and C programming helpful, but not required.
Only one of the following will satisfy the requirements for a degree: CSCE 463 or
CSCE 612. Prerequisite: Graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Additional Course Details
[12.0pt] Because this is a stacked course with CSCE 463, graduate students should
[12.0pt] look for 612-specific sections in each assignment for additional requirements
[12.0pt] and learning opportunities.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
[12.0pt] CSCE 313
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
[12.0pt] At the end of the semester, the students will be able to utilize operating-
[12.0pt] system sockets APIs, create efficient network applications and build multi-
[12.0pt] threaded decentralized frameworks, analyze performance and security of
[12.0pt] networks, develop protocols, and construct models of their system.

# CSCE 624 — Course Syllabus (Spring 2026)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 624.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 624 Syllabus
Section 600 (58435)
Sketch Recognition
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information
Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: TR
Start Time: 12:45PM
End Time: 2:00PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location:  ZACH 590
Credit Hours:  3
[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Tracy Hammond
Email:  hammond@tamu.edu
Office Location:  EAB-C 103
Phone:  979-324-6022
Office Hours
Signup times available on canvas or by request
Preferred Contact Method
Email
[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Sketch Recognition. (3-0). Credit 3. Analysis, implementation, and comparison of
sketch recognition algorithms, including feature-based, vision-based, geometrical,
timing-based, and path-based recognition algorithms; methods for combining these
recognition methods for greater accuracy, using known AI techniques. Prerequisite:
Graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Additional Course Details
Sketch recognition sits at the intersection of artificial intelligence, human–computer
interaction, and visual communication. This course emphasizes  how humans
naturally sketch ,  how systems interpret sketches , and  how recognition
algorithms behave in real-world, noisy settings .
The course is structured around:
hands-on implementation of recognition techniques,
critical reading of classic and contemporary research papers,
in-class activities that build intuition for algorithms and features,
and a semester-long team project that mirrors a research or advanced
development workflow.
The course is discussion-driven and interactive. Students are expected to actively
engage with readings, participate in in-class activities, and reflect on tradeoffs
between algorithmic approaches rather than focusing on a single “correct” solution.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Graduate classification.
Students are expected to have general graduate-level knowledge in computer
science. Familiarity with at least one of the following will be helpful but is not strictly
required:
programming (any language),
basic machine learning or artificial intelligence concepts,
linear algebra or probability,
or software engineering practices.
Students from related disciplines (e.g., engineering, HCI, data science) are welcome
and have historically done well in the course.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Explain core principles of sketch recognition, including data capture,
preprocessing, and feature generation.
Implement and compare multiple sketch recognition approaches, including
feature-based, vision-based, geometric, temporal, and path-based methods.
Apply and evaluate machine learning classifiers in the context of sketch data.
Analyze algorithm performance using appropriate statistical and
experimental evaluation techniques.
Design and conduct sketch-based experiments that account for human
variability, interface design, and data collection constraints.
Critically read, interpret, and discuss research literature in sketch recognition
and related AI/HCI domains.
Collaborate effectively on a team-based project that integrates algorithmic
design, implementation, and evaluation.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Required
No official textbook - material provided by instructor
Notes:
Assigned readings will consist of:
peer-reviewed research papers,
selected book chapters,
and instructor-provided notes.
The course will make use of:
Canvas for announcements, readings, and grades,
Gradescope for assignment submission and feedback,
instructor-provided datasets, code examples, and worksheets,
and AI-based tools where explicitly permitted and documented.
Students may use AI tools (e.g., for coding or ideation)  when explicitly allowed  and
must document how they were used. Undocumented or misrepresented use may
be treated as an academic integrity violation.
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
[12.9pt] Graded Components and Weights
Final grades are based on accumulated points across the semester.
Assignments:  30%
Project (team-based):  30%
Assessments (quizzes and exams):  30%
In-class activities and participation:  10%
Total: 100%
[12.9pt] Grade Scale
A: 90–100
B: 80–<90
C: 70–<80
D: 60–<70
F: < 60
This course is taught to mastery. Grades are not curved.
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
Assignments submitted after the deadline will be accepted with a penalty of  20%
per day late , unless prior arrangements have been made or the absence qualifies
as excused under university policy.
Late work beyond several days may not receive feedback.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
Course Schedule (Tentative)
The course follows a traditional 15-week semester schedule.
Weeks 1–3
Foundations of Sketch Recognition
Sketch data, representation, and preprocessing
Feature generation and human sketch behavior
Weeks 4–5
Data Handling and Evaluation
Data handling and transformation
Recognition models and evaluation
Classifiers, decision strategies, and tradeoffs
Performance metrics and error analysis
Midterm 1:  Thursday, February 12
Weeks 6–7
Core Recognition Approaches
Geometric, temporal, path-based, and hybrid approaches
Statistical reasoning and interpretation
Weeks 8–9
Mathematical and Sequential Models
Linear algebra concepts for recognition
Hidden Markov Models, LLMs, and related techniques
Midterm 2:  Thursday, March 26
Weeks 10–12
Advanced Models and Applications
Advanced and sequential models
Modern and emerging approaches
Applications and case studies
Weeks 13–15
Final Project Integration
Final project presentations
Synthesis and analysis
The schedule reflects the intended flow of topics; specific ordering and pacing may
be adjusted to best support student learning.
[16.0pt] AI Statement
AI tools may be used  when explicitly permitted  for assignments or projects. Any
AI assistance must be clearly documented, including:
what tool was used,
how it was used,
and how the student validated or modified the output.
Students remain fully responsible for the correctness, originality, and understanding
of all submitted work.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Attendance Policy
This course relies heavily on in-class discussion and activities.
Students are expected to attend class regularly and participate actively. If
attendance or participation is graded, expectations and evaluation criteria will be
clearly communicated in advance.
Students who must miss class for an excused reason should notify the instructor as
early as possible.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
Make-up work for excused absences will be handled on a case-by-case basis in
accordance with University Student Rule 7.
Students requesting make-up work must communicate promptly and provide
required documentation. The instructor is not obligated to provide make-up
opportunities for unexcused absences.

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Accessible Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of traditionally disenfranchised
user populations due to disability including discrimination and population
characteristics; review of ethical and legal motivations for creating accessible
technology; universal design and its role in the development, evaluation, design,
and implementation of equitable and inclusive software and computer-based
solutions; application of accessibility standards; compilation of a literature review on
a current topic in accessible computing. Prerequisites: Graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Analyze  common classes of disabilities with respect to culture, identity, and
typical challenges in digital environments, and  propose  appropriate
mitigation strategies.
Justify  the ethical and practical need for accessibility to employers and clients
using evidence-based arguments and effective communication strategies.
Interpret  and  evaluate  key legal frameworks related to disability and
accessibility, and  assess  their implications for technology development.
Compare  and  critique  different models of disability, including the civil rights
model, and  analyze  their impact on approaches to accessibility.
Apply  and  evaluate  relevant accessibility standards in the design and
implementation of technology solutions.
Assess  facilitators and barriers to technology use, and  design  or
recommend  strategies that reduce barriers and support accessibility for
diverse users.
Develop  accessible technologies, documents, and presentations that comply
with established standards, and  evaluate  their effectiveness for people with
disabilities as well as other populations.
Critique  technology, identifying instances where accessibility standards may
be insufficient, and  propose  design alternatives that fulfill the underlying
goals of accessibility.
Design ,  implement , and  evaluate  solutions that enable equitable
interaction, mediating challenges and barriers for specific disabilities, and
justify  design choices within the framework of universal design principles.

None

[16.0pt] Grading Policy
[12.9pt] Graded work modifications
If you want to challenge any grading, you must do so within one week of when the
grade is published.
We reserve the right to audit the grades for any assignments submitted to this
course. During the audit process, we can decrease or increase your score. This
could result in lowering the score of already released grades.
[12.9pt] Grade Weighting
Pre-Class Engagement (CSCE 432: 10%, CSCE 632: 5%)
Pre-class engagement can include but is not limited to course readings, quizzes,
polls, and surveys.
You get credit by submitting engagement submissions prior to the start of
class.
At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add
15% capped at 100%.
This allows you to miss some pre-class engagements without negatively
impacting your score. For example, a friend has a personal crisis, and
you choose to console them instead of doing some pre-class
engagements. You do not have to ask the instructor for special
consideration since it is built into the course.
Group Class Activities (10%)
You will work with people at your table or move to a table so that there are at least
3 people per group.
You will get credit if you attend class and you provide what is required for the
activity.
At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add
15% capped at 100%.
This allows you to miss some group class activities without negatively
impacting your score. For example, you had a flat tire on the way to
campus that caused you to miss a group class activity. You do not have
to ask the instructor for special consideration since it is built into the
course.
Assignments (25%)
You will complete assignments throughout the semester either individually or with
others as specified in the assignment. Note that assignments done with others are
not a part of the group project.
Some assignments will be peer graded.
1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation
of other student assignments.
You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your
peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to
grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)
Quick Applications (20%)
Timed assignments that have you do quick application of class materials. They are
designed to take 15 to 20 minutes while you will have at least 45 minutes to
complete them.
Literature Review (CSCE 432: 0%, CSCE 632: 10%)
Students will compile a literature review on an accessibility topic comprised of at
least 10 related peer reviewed conference or journal papers.
The literature review will clearly lay out
themes and gaps in the research supported by evidence from the papers
an annotated bibliography of each paper cited in the review
Current Research Presentation (CSCE 432: 10%, CSCE 632: 5%)
You will present a summary of HCI research.
Section
432: Your presentation will be based on a recent paper from a refereed
source related to disability/accessibility.
632: Your presentation will be based on your Literature Review (See
above.).
Your presentation will be peer graded.
Grading
This will be peer graded.
Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your
evaluation of other student assignments.
You can lose points for grading significantly differently than
your peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You
will need to grade critically rather than give everyone full
credit.)
Team Project (25%)
You will work with a group to address a barrier encountered by people with
disabilities that does not have an acceptable solution. Your team's barrier must be
approved by the instructor. Teams must design and implement a technology based
solution that provides equitable interactions for the barrier.
Parts of this will be peer graded.
1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation
of other student assignments.
You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your
peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to
grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)
The instructor will establish a multiplier for you based on your team peer
evaluations.
1. The instructor can modify the multiplier at their discretion.
For example, if you are not actively participating with your group
and you are assigned a multiplier of 0.9 and your group’s final
project score is 90, then your score would become 0.9*90 which
is 81.

[12.9pt] Student Behavior & Academic Integrity
Acknowledgement
By submitting anything to this course, electronically or otherwise, you are asserting

unauthorized aid on this academic work. In particular, I certify that I have listed
above all the sources that I consulted regarding this assignment and that I have not

Spirit & Intent
Assignments are designed to help you engage with HCI concepts. Doing anything to

Recording statement
Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student
has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording

permission to record classes are not permitted to redistribute audio or video

recorded.
Plagiarism and Copyright
All course materials are copyrighted and may not be copied or distributed without
permission. Plagiarism will be treated as a serious academic violation per university
policies.
[16.0pt] AI Statement
With the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, our creative processes are
evolving. However, these tools do not replace the essential skills of drafting, revising,
and reflecting on texts, programs, and lectures. Nor do they substitute for the
critical thinking required to locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite sources
responsibly.
Education is about preparing future innovators and leaders. That includes learning
to use emerging technologies ethically and effectively. In this course, you are
permitted to use generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Google Bard, DALL·E 2) to assist
with assignments if you choose to. These tools can help you brainstorm ideas or
provide feedback, but they should not complete your work for you.
To maintain academic integrity:
Cite any AI-generated  content that informs your work. This includes in-text
citations, quotations, and entries in your reference list.
Failure to properly attribute AI-generated material is considered
academic dishonesty .
No more than 30% of any assignment  should consist of AI-generated text.
For guidance on citing  generative AI  using APA 7th edition, refer to the APA Style
Blog for examples, including how to cite ChatGPT in both the body of your work and
your reference list.
Please be aware of the limitations of generative AI:
It often recycles existing ideas rather than producing original thought.
It may present false or misleading information alongside accurate content.
It can hallucinate—creating citations or facts that do not exist.
It may restructure your writing in ways that distort your intended meaning.
Always review and critically evaluate any AI-generated content before including it in
your work.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Attendance Policy
You are required to attend every class, arrive on time, and stay the whole time. You
are responsible for learning the material covered in class, regardless of your
attendance.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
See Course Specific Late Work Policy above.

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Computer Graphics. (3-0). Credit 3. Representation of 3-dimensional objects,
including polyhedral objects, curved surfaces, volumetric representations and CSG
models; techniques for hidden surface/edge removal and volume rendering;
illumination and shading; antialiasing; ray tracing; radiosity; animation; practical
experience with state-of-the-art graphics hardware and software. Prerequisite:
CSCE 441 or approval of instructor. Cross-listed with VIZA 672.
[16.0pt] Additional Course Details
Course overview:  This course provides an in-depth introduction to physically
based rendering, with an emphasis on Monte Carlo techniques. We will study how
light interacts with objects in the real world, how image formation is mathematically
formulated, and how these formulations are solved in practice using modern
rendering algorithms. The course focuses on the theoretical foundations of
rendering as well as their application in practical image synthesis systems.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 441 or approval of instructor. Cross Listing:
VIZA 672/CSCE 641.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Understand the physical and mathematical principles underlying light
transport, image formation, and physically based rendering, including
radiometric quantities and the rendering equation.
Implement core rendering algorithms such as ray tracing, direct illumination,
and global illumination methods.
Analyze the sources of bias and variance in Monte Carlo rendering algorithms
and evaluate the impact of different sampling and acceleration strategies on
rendering quality and performance.
Design and implement a physically based rendering system or rendering
component that integrates multiple techniques covered in the course.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Recommended
Physically Based Rendering: From Theory to Implementation (4th edition)
Authors:  Matt Pharr, Wenzel Jakob, and Greg Humphreys
URL for Resource:  https://pbr-book.org/4ed/contents
This material Is: Recommended
Robust Monte Carlo Methods For Light Transport Simulation
Authors:  Eric Veach
URL for Resource:  https://graphics.stanford.edu/papers/veach_thesis/thesis.pdf
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
Programming assignments (70%):  There will be 4 programming assignments in
C++. One of the assignments is just setting up the environment and finishing a
simple task. So there is only 3 main assignments in practice.
Final project (30%):  Students need to implement additional rendering features not
covered in the assignments. They can work individually or in teams of up to 3
students. Students will propose their project at the middle of the semester. The
final project report will be due on April 30th.
Grading Scale:  A = 90-100, B < 90 and >= 80, C < 80 and >= 70, D < 70 and >= 60, F
< 60
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
You will lose 20% from each assignment for each day that it is late. However, there
will be 5 granted late days for the entire course. You are free to use it for any of the
assignments (note that, you CANNOT use it for the final project!). You will not get
any bonuses for any of the unused late days. All the assignments are due at 11:59
pm on  Canvas  unless otherwise stated. Note that, one minute over and 23 hours
over both count as one full day.

[16.0pt] Additional Course Information
Academic Integrity:  The assignments in this class are individual unless otherwise
stated. For the individual assignments, all code must be written by the student. If
specified in the assignment instructions, the use of external libraries for performing
basic operations is allowed. However, using any external source code is NOT
permitted. Collaborating with other students beyond general discussion is also NOT
allowed. In particular, viewing other students’ code and/or written answers is strictly
prohibited. Posting your code on publicly accessible websites (e.g., GitHub), even
after the assignment deadline, is also NOT allowed.
[16.0pt] AI Statement
The use of large language models such as ChatGPT, Copilot, or Claude is not strictly
prohibited. However, these tools may be used only for debugging or clarification
purposes, not for generating code. You are expected to write all code yourself, and
copying or modifying AI-generated code is not permitted. If you use any AI tool in
any capacity while completing an assignment, even to ask a conceptual question,
you must submit a transcript of the full interaction along with your work.
The instructor may request a meeting with any student to discuss their code and
assess their understanding. If a student is unable to adequately explain their own
code, it will be considered an honor code violation and reported to the honor
council.

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Computational Optimization. (3-0). Credit 3. Combinatorial theory of polytopes as a
tool for the solution of combinatorial optimization problems; applications to max
flow, matching and matroids; geometric interpretation of the results indicating the
profound role that polyhedral combinatorics play in the design and complexity of
approximation algorithms. Prerequisite: CSCE 629.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 629.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Master basic theories and techniques in the current research in computational
optimization.
apply theories and techniques studied in the course to solve real-world problems in
computational optimization.
Use appropriate technologies to communicate, collaborate, and conduct research in
the area of computational optimization.
Gain a solid background and preparation for advanced research in computational
optimization, and other related research areas.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Required
Introduction to Tractability and Approximability of Optimization Problems
Authors:  Jianer Chen
Notes:
Will be posted in Canvas
This material Is: Recommended
The Design of Approximation Algorithms
ISBN:  978-0-521-19527-0
Authors:  D. Williamson and D. Shmoys
Publisher:  Cambridge University Press
Publication Date:  2011
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
There are 3 homework assignments, used to test students’ understanding of
the course lectures. The final exam will be comprehensive. The course
research project will provide students with opportunities to read recent
published papers in the area and do research on problems currently
interesting in the area.
Homework: 10*3 = 30%, Course project: 35%, Final exam 35%.
Grading Scale: A = 90-100%, B = 70-89%, C = 60-70%, F = 0-59 %
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
[11.0pt bold] The assignments and project are due on the designated due dates at the  beginning
of class. No late submissions will be accepted. Discuss unusual circumstances in
advance with the instructor.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
Week 1 (1/12- ): Algorithmic background review
Week 2 (1/19- ): Maximum flow and graph matching
Week 3 (1/26- ): Linear programming
Week 4 (2/2- ): NP-completeness theory and approximation algorithms
Week 5 (2/9- ): Approximation algorithms: FPTAS algorithms, homework #1
due
Week 6 (2/16- ): Approximation algorithms: PTAS algorithms
Week 7 (2/23- ): Approximation algorithms: PTAS algorithms (continued)
Week 8 (3/2- ): Approximation algorithms: APX algorithms
Week 9 (3/9- ): Spring break. No classes
Week 10 (3/16- ): Approximation algorithms: APX algorithms (continued),
homework #2 due
Week 11 (3/23- ): Inapproximability results, PCP theorem
Week 12 (3/30- ): Parameterized algorithms
Week 13 (4/6- ): Parameterized algorithms (continued), homework #3 due
Week 14 (4/13- ): ETH and Computational lower bounds
Week 15 (4/20- ): Exact algorithms for NP-hard problems
Week 16 (4/27): Exact algorithms for NP-hard problems (continued), course
project due
May 1, Friday, Final exam (8:00 am – 10:00 am).
[16.0pt] Additional Course Information
All course information will be posted in canvas. You should check your course
announcements, course material and due dates daily. It is strongly recommended that
you keep up with the course material and do not fall behind. Start working on
assignments as soon as they are assigned.
This course is using Canvas Learning Management System. Students submit their
homework and projects via Canvas.
[16.0pt] AI Statement
[13.0pt] The use of AI is strictly prohibited in this course.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Attendance Policy
[13.0pt] Class attendance is strongly recommended. The student is responsible
[13.0pt] for any material missed. Missed exams may be made up only when the
[13.0pt] absence is university sanctioned. Medical reasons or other
[13.0pt] extenuating conditions beyond the control of the student must be
[13.0pt] properly documented. Discuss unusual circumstances in advance with
[13.0pt] the instructor when possible.

[16.0pt] CSCE 670 Syllabus

Information Storage and Retrieval
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information

Yu Zhang

Office Hours

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Information Storage and Retrieval. (3-0). Credit 3. Representation, storage, and
access to very large multimedia document collections; fundamental data structures
and algorithms of information storage and retrieval systems; techniques to design
and evaluate complete retrieval systems, including cover of algorithms for indexing,
compressing, and querying very large collections. Prerequisites: CSCE 310 or CSCE
603 or approval of instructor; graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 310 or CSCE 603 or approval of instructor;
graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Define and explain the key concepts and models relevant to web search, including
topics like text indexing, retrieval models, evaluation, Web crawling, link-based
algorithms like PageRank, and learning to rank.
• Define and explain the key concepts and models relevant to recommender
systems, including topics like collaborative filtering, matrix factorization,
recommender system evaluation, and implicit recommendation.
• Design, implement, and evaluate the core algorithms underlying a fully functional
web search system and recommendation system.
• Identify the salient features and apply recent research results in web search and
recommender systems, including topics such as adversarial information retrieval and
neural models of retrieval and recommendation.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Optional
Mining of Massive Datasets
Authors:  Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, and Jeffrey D. Ullman
Publication Date:  2014
Notes:
Available online:  http://www.mmds.org/
This material Is: Optional
Introduction to Information Retrieval
Authors:  Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan, and Hinrich Schutze
Publication Date:  2009
Notes:
Available online:  https://nlp.stanford.edu/IR-book/
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
The course grading scale is:
A : >=90%
B : >=80% and < 90%
C : >=70% and < 80%
D : >=60% and < 70%
F : < 60%
[11.0pt bold] Homework 30% : We will have five programming assignments
(2%+7%+7%+7%+7%). All of them will be in Python. The first assignment is an
introductory one designed to help you get familiar with Jupyter Notebook and the
Python operations commonly used in this course.
[11.0pt bold] Quizzes 20% : We will have four brief in-class quizzes (5%+5%+5%+5%).
[11.0pt bold] Final 30% : We will hold a comprehensive final exam from 3:30 - 5:30 PM on May 4
in HRBB 113.
[11.0pt bold] Group Project 20% : Students will work in teams of 3 or 4 to engage in a mini-
research project.
Homework collaboration policy : Homeworks are individual assignments. That is, you
should write your own code, by yourself. However, we do encourage you to talk
amongst yourselves about approaches and methods. As an example, it is
appropriate to sit in a group with no laptops to talk through the problems. It is
inappropriate, however, to pair program, share code, or work through specific
problems in code together. You must acknowledge all help in your homework
submission.
Regrade policy : Once you receive your graded assignment (e.g., a homework
assignment), you have seven days to request a regrade in writing. After seven days,
there will be no regrades. You must give the instructor a written explanation of your
regrade request. We reserve the right to regrade the entire assignment.
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
Homework late day policy : All homeworks are due by 11:59pm on the due date.
Each student gets five late days total. A late day is an indivisible 24-hour unit. For
example, if the due date is 11:59pm on Monday, and you submit at 12:01am
Tuesday, that is 1 late day. Once you use up the five late days, a late assignment will
receive a grade of 0.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
(this is a rough schedule for the course; subject to change)
Week 1: Course Overview; Boolean Retrieval
Week 2: TF-IDF; Vector Space Model; BM25
Week 3: Link Analysis
Week 4: Evaluation of Search Engines
Week 5: Learning to Rank
Week 6: Recommender Systems (Content-Based; Collaborative Filtering)
Week 7: Recommender Systems (Matrix Factorization; Bayesian Personalized
Ranking)
Week 8: word2vec; Neural Ranking
Week 9: Spring Break
Week 10: Transformer; BERT-Based Ranking
Week 11: Neural Collaborative Filtering; Sequential Recommendation
Week 12: Large Language Models Basics
Week 13: Large Language Models with Search Engines
Week 14: Large Language Models for Ranking and Recommendation
Week 15: Wrap-up; Project Presentations
Important Dates
Homeworks
Homework 0: due Jan 26
Homework 1: due Feb 9
Homework 2: due Mar 2
Homework 3: due Mar 30
Homework 4: due Apr 20
Quizzes
Quiz 1: in the Feb 6 class
Quiz 2: in the Feb 27 class
Quiz 3: in the Mar 25 class
Quiz 4: in the Apr 15 class
Group Project
Proposal: due Mar 7
Presentation: in the Apr 20, Apr 22, Apr 24, and Apr 27 classes
Report: due May 2
Final: May 4
[16.0pt] AI Statement
AI policy : In principle you may submit AI-generated code, or code that is based on
or derived from AI-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in
the comments: you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the
response. AI tools may help you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that
the generated code is correct. It is your responsibility to identify errors in program
logic through comprehensive, documented testing. Moreover, generated code,
even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for improvement, in
particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The
submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Computer Supported Collaborative Work. (3-0). Credit 3. Covers design,
implementation and use of technical systems that support people working
cooperatively; draws from the research area of Computer Supported Cooperative
Work (CSCW) and includes current theoretical, practical, technical and social issues
in CSCW and future directions of the field. Prerequisite: CSCE 671 or CSCE 610 or
approval of instructor.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 671 or CSCE 610 or approval of instructor.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
describe the history of asynchronous and synchronous collaborative software
environments
prioritize issues and identify trade-offs in the design of collaborative software
design software capabilities that facilitate awareness, communication and
coordination
analyze use contexts in terms of social processes, time and place settings,
and available resources
assess the need for reciprocity, critical mass, and exception handling for
collaborative applications
design, implement and evaluate software to support collaboration in specific
settings
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Required
online papers from conferences and journals
[16.0pt] Grading Policy

Team project: 35%
Readings write-ups: 35%
Readings presentations: 10%
Attendance (via sign-in sheet): 10%
Grades will be on a 10 point scale: 90-100 = A, 80-89 = B, 70-79 = C, 60-69 = D, and
<60 = F.
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
Write-ups late policy:
50% is immediately deducted from your grade if turned in late. 5% more is
deducted for every day late up to a maximum of one week after the original due
date. For example, if a write up is turned in between 48-72 hours late, the highest
grade it could receive is 40%. Do the reading and turn in the write-ups before class.
Other assignments late policy:
10% is deducted per day late up to a maximum of one week after the original due
date.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
week 1: introductions, CSCW history and central issues
week 2: designing for artifact and human awareness
week 3: forms of communication
week 4: content sharing systems and issues
week 5: collaborative decision making
week 6: voting models and balkanization
week 7: meeting capture and reuse
week 8: crowdsourcing and its issues
week 9: online communities and virtual teams
week 10: shared user history and its visualization
week 11: collaborative search and design
week 12: software supporting social skill development
week 13: collaboration in online games
week 14: supporting software teams

student presentations will occur throughout the semester -- a sign up sheet will be

smaller project activities will be due once per week and readings write-ups will be
due on average twice per week

James Caverlee

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Data Mining and Analysis. (3-0). Credit 3. Broad overview of data mining, integrating
related concepts from machine learning and statistics; exploratory data analysis,
pattern mining, clustering and classification; applications to scientific and online
data. Cross-listed with ECEN 758 and STAT 639.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
I expect all students to have had some previous exposure to basic probability,
statistics, algorithms, and data structures. You should be able to design and
develop large programs and learn new software libraries on your own.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Define and explain the key concepts and models relevant to data mining and
analysis, including data
preparation, normalization, dimensionality reduction, text mining, and so on.
Design, implement, and evaluate data mining algorithms like associate rules,
clustering, anomaly detection, and do so on modern scalable cloud
computing platforms (e.g., Spark).
Identify the salient features and apply recent research results in data mining,
including topics such as graph mining, and large-scale mining.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Required
Course readings will be drawn from a variety of online
textbooks, scholarly papers, and other resources. One
important textbook is listed below.
This material Is: Required
Mining of Massive Data Sets
ISBN:  9781108476348
Authors:  Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David
Ullman
Publisher:  Cambridge University Press
Publication Date:  2020-01-09
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
10% Weekly Assessments
50% Homework
40% Individual Semester-Long Project
A >= 90, B >= 80 and < 90, C >= 70 and < 80, D >= 60 and < 70, F < 60
Weekly Assessments:  Each week will have one or more short quizzes (typically
true/false, multiple choice) over that week's material. These assessments may be
taken multiple times, with only the highest score kept for grading.
Homework:  We will have homework assignments every week. For the first twelve
weeks, these will be worth 4% of your grade. The 13th homework will be worth 2%
of your grade. All homework assignments will be Python notebooks, with a mix of
programming-based problems, written problems, and other questions (e.g., post a
video in response to a particular query, post a response on a Canvas Discussion).
We also require all students to use an AI-coding assistant  (e.g., Cursor, Cline,
CoPilot, etc.). The future of coding (imho) is 5x or 10x developers who can smartly
use AI to accelerate their productivity. So let’s get started practicing now. We make
no expectations that you have been exposed to Python before, but we do expect
you to come up to speed rapidly.
All homework assignments must be submitted by 11:59pm Central time on the due
date. For the homework assignments, you may talk to any other class member or
work in groups to discuss the problems in a general way. However, your actual
detailed solution must be yours alone. If you do talk to other students, you must
write on your assignment who it is that you discussed the problems with. Your
submitted work must be written solely by you and not contain work directly copied
from others.
Homework Collaboration Clarification:  To clarify, your homework is yours alone and
you are expected to complete each homework independently. Your solution should
be written by you without the direct aid or help of anyone else. However, we believe
that collaboration and team work are important for facilitating learning, so we
encourage you to discuss problems and general problem approaches (but not
actual solutions) with your classmates. If you do have a chat with another student
about a homework problem, you must inform us by writing a note on your
homework submission (e.g., Bob pointed me to the relevant section for problem 3).
The basic rule is that no student should explicitly share a solution with another
student (and thereby circumvent the basic learning process), but it is okay to share
general approaches, directions, and so on. If you feel like you have an issue that
needs clarification, feel free to contact either me or the TA.
[11.0pt bold] Individual Semester-Long Project:  Over the course of the semester, you will work
on a significant data mining project. The project is broken into five major
deliverables:
1. Data Selection (Feb 3)
2. Initial Research Question (Mar 5)
3. Deep Dive (Apr 2)
4. Project Showcase  (week of Apr 20)
5. Final Deliverable (Apr 27)
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
Late homework assignments will be penalized 1% per hour after the deadline, up to
a maximum of 48 hours. After 48 hours, the assignment will receive a 0. To
illustrate, an assignment submitted at 5:05am will be penalized 6 points (so it will be
graded out of 94, instead of 100).
For the Individual Semester-Long Project, students will have FIVE late days in total
for the first three deliverables -- Data Selection (Feb 3), Initial Research Question
(Mar 5), and Deep Dive (Apr 2). The final two deliverables -- Project Showcase (week
of Apr 20) and Final Deliverable (Apr 27) -- will not be accepted late.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
Week 1 :: Course introduction
Week 2 :: Frequent Itemset Mining
Week 3 :: Graph Mining
Week 4 :: Graph Mining and Large-Scale ML
Week 5 :: Large-Scale ML
Week 6 :: Clustering
Week 7 :: Text Mining
Week 8 :: Data Mining and Distributed Computing
Week 9 :: Anomaly Detection
Week 10 :: Graph Embeddings
Week 11 :: Locality-Sensitive Hashing
Week 12 :: Stream Mining
Week 13 :: Hot Topics in Data Mining
Week 14 :: Project Showcase
Week 15 :: Finalize Project Deliverables
Major Project Deadlines:
1. Data Selection (Feb 3)
2. Initial Research Question (Mar 5)
3. Deep Dive (Apr 2)
4. Project Showcase (week of Apr 20)
5. Final Deliverable (Apr 27)

[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Data Visualization. (3-0). Credit 3. Visual representation and design of data and
information; 3D visualization, infographics, data narratives, principles of visual data
encoding, and interaction techniques. Prerequisites: Graduate classification or
approval of instructor. Cross-listed with VIZA 676.
[16.0pt] Additional Course Details
This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the field of data visualization
and visual analytics, guiding students through both foundational concepts and
advanced techniques in visual representation. Beginning with an overview of data
types and visual analytic tasks, students will explore key frameworks, including the
Nested Model of Visual Analytics and principles of marks and channels. Advanced
topics covered include multivariate, sequential, spatial, network, and text
visualization, and the integration of multiple views for more complex datasets.
Innovative areas like data storytelling, augmented and virtual reality (AR/VR) for data,
and the role of data visualization in Explainable AI, as well as e-learning, are also
discussed, offering students a forward-thinking perspective on the field. Through
hands-on tutorials in D3 and Vega-Lite and later in web programming, students will
gain practical skills for crafting interactive visualizations. Students will present
project pitches, receive feedback, and collaborate on projects, culminating in final
presentations.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Graduate classification or approval of instructor.
Cross Listing: VIZA 676/CSCE 679.
There will be assignments and projects that involve programming in
HTML/CSS/JavaScript for the UI part, and Python for data and AI. While having
programming experience in both is great, it is not required.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
At the end of the course, students will be able to:
Analyze diverse data types (e.g., categorical, quantitative, temporal, spatial,
network, and text data) and map visual analytical tasks to appropriate visual
marks and encoding channels.
Compare, select, and justify visualization techniques for multivariate,
sequential, spatial, network, and text data based on data characteristics,
analytical goals, and perceptual effectiveness.
Apply the Nested Model for Visualization Design and established Rules of
Thumb to design, implement, and refine visual analytic systems.
Evaluate state-of-the-art data visualization applications in domains such as
data storytelling, immersive analytics (AR/VR), explainable AI, e-learning, and
digital twins, using design principles and empirical evidence.
Design and develop an end-to-end data visualization project on a topic of the
student’s choice, including problem formulation, data preparation,
visualization design, implementation, and evaluation.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Recommended
Visualization Analysis and Design
Authors:  Tamara Munzner

[12.0pt] Students are required to complete a course project over the course of the
[12.0pt] semester. Undergraduate students may choose any tools or platforms for
[12.0pt] implementation. Graduate students must implement their project using the
[12.0pt] D3.js visualization library: 50%

# CSCE 681 — Course Syllabus (Spring 2026)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 681.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 681 Syllabus
Section 600 (12644)
Seminar
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information
Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: SEM
Meeting Days: TR
Start Time: 12:45PM
End Time: 2:00PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location:  EABB 106
Credit Hours:  1
[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Ricardo Gutierrez-Osuna
Email:  rgutier@tamu.edu
Office Location:  PETR 309
Phone:  979-845-29-42
Office Hours
By apointment
Preferred Contact Method
csce-681-rgo@tamu.edu
[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Seminar. Credit 0 to 1. Reports and discussion of current research and of selected
published technical articles. May not be taken for credit more than once in master's
degree program nor twice in PhD program. Prerequisite: Graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
[12.0pt] The purpose of the course is to expose students to a broad range of current
[12.0pt] and seminal research topics in computer science and related fields. Upon
[12.0pt] completion of the course, students should be able to prepare reports and
[12.0pt] discuss research works and selected published technical articles.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
[12.0pt] To pass this course, you must
[12.0pt] 1. complete the syllabus quiz on canvas with a grade of 100%, and
[12.0pt] 2. receive an average score of 80% or higher.
[12.0pt] Any course requirements completed before passing the syllabus quiz (i.e.,
[12.0pt] 100% score) available on Canvas will not be considered.
[12.0pt bold] Section 600:
[12.0pt] You are expected to
[12.0pt] attend all the lectures scheduled during the semester, and
[12.0pt] write a report for each of them
[12.0pt] Missed reports without a proper Excused Absence will receive an
[12.0pt] automatic score of zero.
[12.0pt] Reports must be typed on a Canvas quiz and require LockDown
[12.0pt] Browser to avoid use of external aids (e.g., AI writing tools).
[12.0pt] Report quizzes open at the start of each lecture (12:45pm) and close at
[12.0pt] the end of the lecture (2:00pm). We suggest you take notes during the
[12.0pt] lecture (e.g., on paper), and then polish the report after the Q&A, up
[12.0pt] until the quiz closes.
[12.0pt bold] Each report quiz will   include five sections: (1) background, (2) problem
[12.0pt bold] statement, (3) proposed solution , (4) results, and (5) critique.   Each
[12.0pt] section is worth one point. Thus, to get a score of 80% for each report,
[12.0pt] you must receive a score of at least 4 out of 5 points.
[12.0pt] You must bring your TAMU ID to the seminar so we can verify you
[12.0pt] attended the lecture.
[12.0pt] Other forms of identification (e.g., driver's license) will NOT be
[12.0pt] accepted.
[12.0pt] Please come to the seminar room 5-10 minutes early so we can
[12.0pt] scan your ID.
[12.0pt] If you arrive late (2+ minutes after the start time), you can still
[12.0pt] attend the lecture but your ID will not be scanned and your
[12.0pt] attendance will not be verified.
[12.0pt] Reports submitted without verified attendance will receive a score
[12.0pt] of zero.
[12.0pt] Section 700:
[12.0pt] You are expected to
[12.0pt] watch the recordings of all the lectures scheduled during the
[12.0pt] semester, and
[12.0pt] write a report for each of them
[12.0pt] Lecture recordings will be available on Canvas, typically within 2-4
[12.0pt] hours after the lecture ends. You will be able to watch the lecture
[12.0pt] recording at your leisure.
[12.0pt] Report quizzes will also be available on Canvas, typically within 24
[12.0pt] hours after the lecture ends.  Report quizzes require LockDown Browser
[12.0pt] and are time limited (<15 minutes), so be sure to watch the recording in
[12.0pt] advance and take notes.
[12.0pt] Report quizzes must be submitted on Canvas by the end of the week
[12.0pt] (i.e., Sunday midnight).
[12.0pt] Each report quiz will consist of a series of multiple-choice, multiple-
[12.0pt] answer questions related to the contents of the lecture.  The number of
[12.0pt] questions may vary from lecture to lecture depending on the contents of
[12.0pt] the lecture.  The value of each quiz question (i.e., number of points) will
[12.0pt] vary depending on the complexity of the question, and the total score
[12.0pt] will be normalized to a 5-point scale.
[12.0pt bold] Final grade.  Your final grade will be the statistical mean of the top 75% of the
[12.0pt] individual quiz grades, i.e., the lowest 25% of your quiz grades do not count,
[12.0pt] regardless of whether you completed them or not. This gives you a bit of
[12.0pt] flexibility: a few low quiz grades may not affect your grade, and you may miss
[12.0pt] a few unexcused lectures (e.g., you arrived late to class, your battery died,
[12.0pt] connection issues).
[12.0pt] Example: Assume we have 12 lectures during the semester (the actual
[12.0pt] number is likely to be higher). Further, assume you received the following
[12.0pt] scores on the 12 lectures: [5.0, 4.5, 4.5, 4, 4, 4, 4, 3.5, 3.0, 2.0, 0.0, 0.0].
[12.0pt] Your final score will be 4.05, which is the average of the top 75% of your
[12.0pt] grades (i.e., we dropped the lowest 3 scores.)
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
[12.0pt] Late work is not accepted under any circumstances.  Please review the
[12.0pt] grading policy.
[16.0pt] Course Schedule
[12.0pt] The course schedule is available as a  spreadsheet .  Please review
[12.0pt] periodically as new speakers are added throughout the semester.
[16.0pt] Additional Course Information
[12.0pt] ATTENDANCE AND ETIQUETTE (SECTION 600 ONLY)
[12.0pt] Please reserve the entire scheduled time slot, and plan to arrive at least 10
[12.0pt] minutes before the start of the talk, since we will be taking class attendance.
[12.0pt] For this purpose, you are required to have your Aggie ID card with you.
[12.0pt] During the seminar, you are expected to pay attention and refrain from
[12.0pt] activities such as doing other work, reading the newspaper, surfing the web,
[12.0pt] or sleeping. Behavior such as this gives speakers a bad impression of our
[12.0pt] graduate program and students. Students noticed exhibiting such behavior
[12.0pt] will not receive credit for attending that seminar.
[12.0pt] In some cases, the seminars will finish a few minutes early. If the speaker is
[12.0pt] not finished, or if the Q&A period is ongoing, you are expected to stay until
[12.0pt] the end of the talk.
[12.0pt] EXCUSED ABSENCES
[12.0pt] The grading criteria (we drop the bottom 25% of your report grades) avoids
[12.0pt] much of the need to request excused absences. If you want to request an
[12.0pt] excused absence, please follow these steps:
[12.0pt] Go to  Student Rule 7  to determine if your situation is covered, then
[12.0pt] Send an email to  csce-681-rgo@tamu.edu  with:
[12.0pt] A description of your situation, and
[12.0pt] Which provision within Student Rule 7 applies
[12.0pt] Supporting documentation (doctor's note, cover letter, etc.)
[12.0pt] Your request will be reviewed, and you will be notified of its
[12.0pt] approval/rejection via email
[12.0pt] Timeline for submitting excused absences : Per  Student Rule 7 , you must
[12.0pt] make the request prior to the day of absence. If this is not possible, you
[12.0pt] must provide notification by the end of the second business day after the
[12.0pt] last date of the absence, including an explanation of why advance notice
[12.0pt] could not be sent. Requests for excused absences that do not meet these
[12.0pt] guidelines will not be considered.
[12.0pt] Missed lectures with an approved excused absence will not be counted
[12.0pt] towards the final grade. Missed lectures without an approved excused
[12.0pt] absence will receive an automated score of zero.
[16.0pt] AI Statement
[12.0pt] Seminar reports containing plagiarized content (including self-plagiarism or
[12.0pt] use of any machine-generated text or translation tools) will be submitted to
[12.0pt] the Aggie Honors Office, with the recommendation that the student receives
[12.0pt] an F* for the course.. Please review the TAMU guidelines for  Academic
[12.0pt] Integrity and Plagiarism .
[12.0pt] Occasionally, we find evidence of plagiarism after the reports have been
[12.0pt] graded. For example, a student may submit a report that has been
[12.0pt] plagiarized from a prior submission, or a prior report may be flagged when a
[12.0pt] new detector of AI-generated text becomes available. For these reasons, you
[12.0pt] should treat grades for seminar reports as provisional. Any prior submission
[12.0pt] that is later found to constitute a violation will be submitted to the Aggie
[12.0pt] Honors Office, with the same recommendation that the student receives an
[12.0pt bold] F* .
[12.0pt] Any information (e.g., a doctor’s note for an excused absences) that is found
[12.0pt] to have been fabricated, falsified and/or misused will be submitted to the
[12.0pt bold] Aggie Honors Office with the recommendation the student receives an  F*  for
[12.0pt] the course.
[16.0pt] Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
[12.0pt] Makeup work will not be allowed given that the grading policy provides
[12.0pt] flexibility for missed lectures and/or poor grades on certain report quizzes.

# CSCE 713 — Course Syllabus (Spring 2026)
# This document is the official course syllabus for CSCE 713.

<!-- body_font:11.0pt -->
[16.0pt] CSCE 713 Syllabus
Section 600 (58633)
Software Security
Spring 2026 - College Station
[16.0pt] Course Information
Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: MWF
Start Time: 08:00AM
End Time: 08:50AM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location:  EABB 106
Credit Hours:  3
[16.0pt] Instructor Details
Marcus Botacin
Email:  botacin@tamu.edu
Office Location:  PETR 224
Phone:  (979) 458-6850
Office Hours
MWF 9AM (right after the class)
Preferred Contact Method
By email
Webpage:  https://marcusbotacin.github.io/
[16.0pt] Catalog Description
Software Security. (3-0). Credit 3. Basic principles of design and implementation of
secure software, code reviews including tool-assisted review by static and dynamic
analysis, risk analysis and management, and methods for software security testing.
Prerequisites: CSCE 431 or CSCE 606 or approval of instructor; graduate
classification.
[16.0pt] Course Prerequisites
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 431 or CSCE 606 or approval of instructor;
graduate classification.
CSCE 431 or CSCE 606 or approval of instructor; graduate classification.
[16.0pt] Course Learning Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
[11.0pt bold] list  the first principles of security and  explain  why each is important to
security and how it enables the development of security mechanisms that
can implement desired security policies.
[11.0pt bold] identify  specific principles that have been violated in common security
failures.
[11.0pt bold] identify  appropriate design principles to apply in each software development
scenario.
[11.0pt bold] explain  the interaction between security and system usability and
importance of human-computer interfaces to system usability.
[11.0pt bold] explain  the importance of secure software and the programming practices,
development processes, and methodologies that lead to secure software.
[11.0pt bold] explain  techniques for specifying program behavior, the classes of well-
known defects, and how they manifest themselves in various languages.
[11.0pt bold] perform  penetration testing on previously unknown software.
[11.0pt bold] analyze  existing source code for functional correctness.
[11.0pt bold] analyze  software for defects using industry standard tools.
[11.0pt bold] develop  test cases that demonstrate the existence of defects.
[11.0pt bold] develop  defect-free software components that satisfy their functional
requirements.
[16.0pt] Special Course Designation
None
[16.0pt] Textbook and/or Resource Materials
This material Is: Optional
Provided by the Instructor
Notes:
No textbook is required. All materials will be posted on the class website (Canvas). Students
should expect to run security tools on their own machines. This course follows the minimum
device requirements established by the College of Engineering
( https://engineering.tamu.edu/academics/byod/devices/index.html ).
[16.0pt] Grading Policy
The grading is composed of:
Vulnerabilities PoC* (Individual): 50%
First project** (*Groups of 4 students): 25%
Final project*** (*Groups of 4 students): 25%
*Group size might be adjusted to accommodate a large number of students
enrolled in the course, at the instructor’s discretion. Ask for permission.
Independent-Study Reports**** (optional, individual): +10%
No written exam, no quiz.
Total: 100%
[11.0pt bold] *Vulnerabilities PoC
Students are required to deliver PoCs for the vulnerabilities explained in class by the
instructor. PoCs should be an end-to-end exploit of the sample applications provided
by the instructor.
The PoC is considered successful if it meets all the requirements below:
The student delivers a source code for the PoC.
The student delivers a video demonstrating the exploitation at the student
machine.
The exploitation can be reproduced by the instructor/TA.
[11.0pt bold] **First Project
Each group should:
Students should identify and exploit an exploitable Web vulnerability in a
Web-based project available on GitHub.
The vulnerability should not have been reported before in any sense (i.e., no
known CVEs)
The students should responsibly disclose the vulnerabilities to the project
creators according to the instructions provided in the lecture on the topic.
Projects without proper disclosure will not be considered.
The exploitation should be done locally (e.g., in Docker) and not in a live
system. Exploitation attempts causing actual harm will not be allowed.
The students should deliver a PoC and a video for the exploitation, as with
the traditional PoC assignments.
The students should deliver a presentation report in class on the final
project dates.
[11.0pt bold] ***Final Project
Each group should:
Students should identify and exploit an exploitable non-Web vulnerability in
a real application,
Web applications are not valid for the final project.
The same rules as for the First project apply.
[11.0pt bold] **** Independent Self-Study
Students are encouraged to deliver a 1-page summary of known vulnerabilities from
the literature, upon the instructor’s suggestion. The evaluation is as follows:
Identifying pre-conditions: 10%
Technical Explanation: 50%
Remediation Explanation: 30%
Contextualization with other class topics: 10%
Topics:
The topics will include vulnerabilities pointed out by the instructor in Canvas
and all final project presentations by all teams.
Grading Scale
[11.0pt bold] Grading Scale
90% <= A
80% <= B < 89%
70% <= C < 79%
60% <= D < 69%
F <60%
[16.0pt] Late Work Policy
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
[16.0pt] Course Specific Late Work Policy
Extensions/make-ups can be given 12 hours prior to the deadline upon written/in-person
justification request. 20min will be given as a grace period. Otherwise, 50% off from the
credit you got for that submission.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late
work and is exempted from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).
